extra.message: User-controlled 'code' parameter from the registration form is directly interpolated into a raw SQL query via f-string and text(). The 'code' value from form.registration_code.data flows unmodified into text(f"...WHERE code = '{code}'") at line 17, enabling SQL injection. An attacker can bypass registration code validation or extract data from the database.

User-controlled 'search' query parameter from request.args.get('search', '') at line 27 is directly interpolated into a raw SQL condition via f-string and text() at line 33. The search_param value flows unmodified into text(f"text like '%{search_param}%'"), enabling SQL injection to read, modify, or delete database contents.

User-controlled request.path is interpolated into an f-string whose result is passed to render_template_string(). Since render_template_string() evaluates Jinja2 template expressions in the string, an attacker can inject template syntax (e.g., {{config}}, {{''.__class__.__mro__}}) via the URL path to achieve Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) leading to remote code execution.

The download() function passes a user-supplied URL directly to urllib.request.urlopen() without any validation or allowlist. The URL originates from form.url.data (routes/account.py:60-61), which is submitted via the 'Change Image' form. An attacker can use this to perform Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) against internal services (e.g., http://localhost:8080, http://169.254.169.254/ for cloud metadata).

The 'preferences' cookie value is base64-decoded and deserialized using pickle.loads() without any integrity check. Since pickle deserialization can execute arbitrary Python code, an attacker who controls the 'preferences' cookie (which is user-controlled via HTTP headers) can achieve remote code execution on the server by crafting a malicious pickle payload.

The delete_note endpoint retrieves a note by note_id from the URL and deletes it without verifying that note.user_id matches current_user.id. Any authenticated user can delete any other user's notes by simply changing the note_id in the URL. An admin can already delete any note via the template (home.html:41), but no ownership check exists for non-admin users either.

cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

extra.message: The get_personal_notes endpoint accepts a user_id from the URL path and returns all notes for that user, including private ones (no private=False filter). There is no check that user_id == current_user.id. Any authenticated user can view any other user's private notes by changing the user_id in /accounts/<user_id>/notes.

cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

extra.message: The /account POST route (update_account) lacks the @login_required decorator. The AccountForm includes an 'is_admin' BooleanField (forms/account_form.py:8), and the handler at lines 76-81 copies all form data (including is_admin) into current_user.__dict__ without any admin privilege check. A regular authenticated user can escalate to admin by submitting is_admin=True in the POST body to /account.

User-controlled note text is rendered with the 'safe' Jinja2 filter ({{ note.text | safe }}), which disables HTML escaping. Note text is submitted by users via the create note form and stored in the database. When displayed, any embedded HTML/JavaScript will execute in the browser of any user viewing the note, including administrators.

The render_table helper is called with safe_columns=['text'] which marks the 'text' column as safe (no HTML escaping). User-controlled note text will be rendered as raw HTML, enabling stored XSS. The same pattern exists in templates/personal_notes.html:16. Additionally, the search query value is rendered at line 9 (value="{{search}}") enabling reflected XSS via the search parameter.

Flask secret_key is hardcoded to 'super secret key' in the source code. This weak, publicly-known secret undermines Flask's session signing mechanism, enabling attackers to forge session cookies and impersonate any user.

The /account POST route (update_account) is missing the @login_required decorator, unlike the /account GET route (line 18) which has it. Any unauthenticated user can POST to this endpoint. Combined with the mass-assignment of form fields to current_user attributes (line 81), this creates a privilege escalation vector.

cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

extra.message: All form data fields are bulk-assigned to current_user.__dict__ via .update() without filtering sensitive attributes. The AccountForm includes is_admin (BooleanField), password, password_control, and email fields. While 'password' is excluded, 'is_admin' is not. A non-admin user can submit is_admin=True to escalate privileges, or set arbitrary User model attributes by adding unexpected form fields.

cwe: CWE-915: Improperly Controlled Modification of Dynamically-Determined Object Attributes
